Iran’s strategy all along has been to impose an unbearable economic cost on the US if Trump continued the war via oil and the closure of the Strait of Hormuz.

Now, oil is down ~20% well below $100 a barrel. From that perspective, it’s clear who benefits most from the 2-week ceasefire off ramp. That’s Trump. 

But the question is: would Tehran extract significant concessions in the negotiations in terms of sanctions relief and/or somehow Hormuz control? Perhaps that’s the quid pro quo.

BREAKING: Iran also confirms a two-week ceasefire. But the reopening of the Strait of Hormuz is somewhat muddled, with a warning of “technical limitations” and the need of “coordination” with the Iranian military. Still, it re-opens the flow of oil and LNG.
Interesting that Iran also somehow recognises the U.S. 15-point plan as part of the peace negotiations.

BREAKING: US President Trump agrees to 2-week ceasefire provided Iran agrees, too, to re-open the Strait of Hormuz. “This will be a double side ceasefire,” he says. 

Importantly, Trump also says a 10-point proposal from Iran is a “workable” basis to negotiate permanent peace. https://t.co/uR9bRnXxcn

Pakistan Prime Minister: “… To allow diplomacy to run its course, I earnestly request President Trump to extend the deadline for 2 weeks. Pakistan, in all sincerity, requests the Iranian brothers to open Strait of Hormuz for a corresponding period of 2 weeks as a goodwill….”

The world’s most important price for physical oil barrels soared to its highest on record. Dated Brent, which helps price much of the world’s actual oil barrels, hit $144.42 on Tuesday, according to Platts, a unit of S&P Global Energy.
Of course, that’s in nominal terms; in real terms, adjusted by cumulative inflation, Dated Brent would need to rise to >$220 a barrel to surpass the level it hit in 2008.

MUST READ: “General Caine […] also flagged the enormous difficulty of securing the Strait of Hormuz and the risks of Iran blocking it. Mr. Trump had dismissed that possibility on the assumption that the regime would capitulate before it came to that.”

The oil futures market continues to price a big TACO.

Physical oil quotes are sky high, but futures imply a significant improvement in supply in a few weeks.

If the market moves to priced a much longer disruption — and see below — then oil futures will rise significantly.

The US Energy Information Administration (@EIAgov) is now forecasting a rather long disruption to crude oil production in the Persian Gulf.

(Note this is wellhead production rather than flows via Strait of Hormuz. It doesn't appear to include natural gas liquids, so just crude) https://t.co/JxRL8nEMli
(Note the @EIAgov forecast/model closed April 2)

Brent (physical and futures) still pricing for a short war

Brent prices have surged into a record backwardation as the United States and Iran threaten to escalate their conflict by attacking energy infrastructure on both sides of the Persian Gulf.

In the futures market, Brent’s six-month calendar spread has traded in an average backwardation of more than $26 per barrel so far this month, the highest on record in nominal terms, up from less than $1 in December.

After adjusting for inflation, the six-month futures spread is on course to exceed the previous record set in October 1990, after Iraq’s invasion of Kuwait and as the United States and its allies massed forces in the Gulf ...

This is a short extract from my newsletter emailed to clients earlier today. If you would like to read my newsletters in full, three times a week, you can subscribe via my website https://t.co/YjJTctVocX

Brent bulls focus on escalation not mediation

Investors remain exceptionally bullish about the outlook for Brent as prices grind higher and the United States and Iran show no indication of ending the conflict that has slowed exports from the Persian Gulf to a trickle.

Both sides continue to present maximalist demands in public and attack energy infrastructure on both sides of the Gulf despite attempts at mediation by diplomats from Pakistan and other regional countries.

Hedge funds and other money managers purchased the equivalent of 22 million barrels in Brent futures and options over the seven days ending on March 31, reversing a similar volume of sales the week before ...

AUSTRALIA’s coal export prices have risen by around 25-30% compared with the start of the year. Front-month Newcastle coal futures have averaged $137 per tonne so far in April up from $108 in January. Electricity generators across South and East Asia are likely to increase coal-fired generation to conserve scarce gas supplies following the loss of LNG from the Middle East. But China and India (the region’s top two coal generators) are expected to meet some of the extra fuel from domestic mines which explains the relatively small increase in coal prices so far:

Man, even if you agree to a ceasefire for 2 weeks and the Strait of Hormuz is open for 2 weeks, that drains the 180 million bbls of floating storage, but very few tankers will go into the Strait during that time.

The production shut-in continues.
This is set already. Even if the Strait opens this very second, these draws are coming.

If you don't want to be clueless about physical oil trading, you should follow @OilCfd. 

Amazing follow.

Here's an early glimpse of what's to come for US commercial crude storage. By May 8 week, if tanker flows are what they are, imports will drop, and exports will spike.

US SPR will have to be 10 million bbls to offset some of the draws we are going to see. https://t.co/zTR1CxVI46

Onshore inventories are about to plummet.

US crude exports are about to skyrocket. 

The US is the last place to draw.

To the casual observer, please don't follow EIA's forward-looking projections.

EIA is so bad at modeling oil market balances that it can't even model historical ones correctly.

For God's sake, and I can't even believe I'm saying this, at least the IEA revises up demand assumptions. 

EIA's forward projections are about as useless as looking at the wind direction outside and wondering where the stock market is headed.

And everyday that passes, it’s harder to go back to normal.

The leaks were interesting. 

First, Pakistan was making effort in brokering the deal. “It was close” apparently.

Now after President Trump’s social post, Iran has cut off all diplomatic relationships. 

Meanwhile, similar reports from before were saying White House officials were worried about spikes in energy prices starting “next week” or the “breaking point” in the oil market. 

I guess today is the ultimatum.
Heads up that if that Strait *actually* reopens, ships will be crossing with their AIS transponders on so we can cut it with all the contested dark (ie AIS off) transit  estimates.

It’s gonna get real interesting, folks!

Dated Brent was at ~$105 in April 2014, before the BIG shale-induced rout in crude prices.

Adjusting for inflation, that's roughly where we are today.

And the Strait of Hormuz remains closed.

"yeah, well, in 2008 the inflation adjusted crude price high was over $200, so this is nothing!" https://t.co/Bk9ApSEeLA
There it is—Dated Brent (i.e., spot) crude hits $144.46/bbl, a new all-time high. https://t.co/ZbyBzpTStQ

To be clear, that's a *nominal* fresh all-time high, which outstrips the $144.22/bbl the global crude benchmark reached in 2008.

Obviously we've had a lot of inflation since then, so the 2008 high is more like $216/bbl in current dollars

Next onto the inflation-adjusted record?

President Donald Trump said that the United States is in “heated negotiations” over the Iran war, declining to elaborate on the talks.

“I can’t tell you, because right now we’re in heated negotiations,” Trump told Fox News in a brief phone interview when asked how he’s feeling about talks.

Trump said that he was about to be fully briefed on Pakistani Prime Minister Shehbaz Sharif’s proposed two-week ceasefire — to which the White House told CNN earlier Tuesday “a response will come.”

“I can say this — that I know him very well. He’s a highly respected man, all over,” Trump told Fox.  https://t.co/5zVghO2lRC

Iranians assembled at power plants and bridges Tuesday, according to videos and photos posted by state media, following President Trump’s threats to wipe out Iranian civilization unless a deal is reached with Tehran.

People appeared to stand outside of a power plant and formed a “human chain” along the White Bridge in Ahvaz, according to videos shared by Iran's semi-official Fars News Agency and verified by Storyful, which is owned by News Corp, the parent company of The Wall Street Journal. https://t.co/c9zAIVvyBw
